title: CHAPTER II.
summary: Gordon Peterson was a young man living in New Bedford, England, in 1827. He was sent by his father to spend a fortnight with a relative in Bedford. When he returned he found his grandfather waiting for him at the corner of a street. "I'd want you for to know better, you blackguard, than to call my new obercoat a darty one!"

title: CHAPTER III.
summary: A friend of his sent him a slip of paper with some words written on it. The words were written in red ink, and appeared to say: "Blood--your life depends upon lying close" The writer tried to read them all at once, but could not. "I could think of nothing affording a satisfactory solution of the mystery of my situation"

title: CHAPTER IV.
summary: The captain of the brig, Augustus Barnard, was imprisoned in his cabin. He was about to ascend when he was attacked by a gang of mutineers. The men below could not be induced to venture on deck by threats or promises. They were eventually forced to give up their arms. A scene of the most horrible butchery ensued.

title: CHAPTER V.
summary: Augustus had been imprisoned for ten days in the hold of the ship's forecastle. He was freed when Dirk Peters came below, when Tiger the dog leaped into the berth and lay down. Overjoyed at discovering me to be still alive, he now relentlessly resolved to brave every difficulty and danger in reaching me.

title: CHAPTER VI.
summary: The captain of the brig, Augustus, was forced to hide in the hold of the ship. The brig was carrying a party of mutineers who were resolved to turn pirates. He spent the next eight days hiding in a box in the forecastle. Tiger was able to escape through a hole in his jacket.

title: CHAPTER VII.
summary: Hartman Rogers died on the eighth after drinking a glass of grog. He told Augustus that he believed the mate had poisoned him, and that he expected, if he did not be on the ship, his own turn would come shortly. Peters expressed his determination of attempting to take the vessel, provided Augustus would lend him his aid. Peters stripped off the shirt of the corpse and dropped the body overboard.

title: CHAPTER VIII.
summary: Three of us crept to the cabin companion-way. Only one was asleep, and he lying just at the foot of the companion-ladder. The rest were seated on several mattresses, which had been taken from the berths. All had knives, one or two of them pistols, and a great manymuskets were lying in a berth close at hand. The captain of the brig was ordered to take up his quarters in the cabin, and be one of them for the future.

title: CHAPTER IX.
summary: The four of us had lashed ourselves firmly to the fragments of the windlass. We had little hope that Augustus would recover, as he evinced no signs of life. He had merely swooned from loss of blood, the bandages we had placed around his wounded arm having been torn off by the water.

title: CHAPTER X.
summary: A large vessel bearing down upon us, and not more than a couple of miles off. Augustus, Peters and Parker were equally affected, although in different ways. The bodies of the woman were so badly decomposed that it was impossible to make out her character. From the saffron-like hue of the corpses, we concluded that the whole of her company had perished by the yellow fever.

title: CHAPTER XI.
summary: We spent the remainder of the day in a condition of stupid lethargy, watching the retreating vessel until the darkness, hiding her from our senses, recalled us. The pangs of hunger and thirst then returned, absorbing all other cares andconsiderations. Nothing, however, could be done until the morning, and, securing ourselves as well as possible, we endeavoured to snatch a bottle of Port.

title: CHAPTER XII.
summary: I had secretly made up my mind to.uffer death in any shape or under any circumstances rather than resort.to such a course. The proposition had not been heard by either Peters or Augustus. I therefore took Parker by the throat and prayed to God for power to dissuade him from the horrible purpose he entertained. We were to draw lots to see if the ship we had seen might be again in sight. Small splinters of wood were made to answer our purpose.

title: CHAPTER XIII.
summary: The crew of a ship in the middle of the Atlantic were stranded in a gale. The ship was at the mercy of every wind and wave. The crew tried to get water from the storeroom but were unable to do so. Two jars of our olives, as well as the whole of our ham, were washed overboard.

title: CHAPTER XIV.
summary: The Jane Guy was a fine-looking topsail schooner of a hundred and eighty tons burden. She was unusually sharp in the bows, and on a wind, in moderate weather, the fastest sailer I have ever seen. But her draught of water was by far too great for the trade to which she was destined.

title: CHAPTER XV.
summary: The islands of Tristan d'Acunha, in latitude 37° 8' S., longitude 12° 12' W, were first discovered by the Portuguese, and was visited by the Dutch in 1643. In 1811, a Captain Heywood, in the Nereus, visited Tristan. He found there three Americans, who were residing upon the islands.

title: CHAPTER XVI.
summary: Palmer's Land is south of the Shetland, latitude sixty-four degrees. It is bounded on the north by Georgia, east by Sandwich Land and the South Orkneys. The Royal Geographical Society of London concluded that there is a continuous tract of land extending from 47° 30' E. to 69° 29' W.

title: CHAPTER XVII.
summary: The crew of a ship off the coast of New Zealand were trying to reach an iceberg. The ice was too thick for the ship to pass, and the crew were forced to abandon the ship. The crew were unable to reach the island because of heavy snowfall. Captain Guy urged the crew to continue on their way to the pole.

title: CHAPTER XVIII.
summary: Captain Guy and his crew set sail for the Gallipagos Islands. They found a landlocked bay, completely landlocked, on the southeastern coast of the main island. There were three fine springs of good water, and abundance of wood in the vicinity. Captain Guy decided to stay a week to recruit, and then push on to the southward.

title: CHAPTER XIX.
summary: We were nearly three hours in reaching the village, it being more than three miles in the interior, and the path lying through a ruggedcountry. The dwellings were of the most miserable description imaginable, and, like those of even the lowest of the savage races, were of no uniform plan. There were a great variety of tame fowls running about, and these seemed to be the chief food of the natives.

title: CHAPTER XX.
summary: The tortoises were as fine as we had ever seen, and the ducks surpassed our best species of wild fowl. In return for these good things we presented the natives with blue beads, nails, knives, and pieces of red cloth. We established a regular market on the shore, just under the guns of the schooner. The Chinese consider _biche de mer_ a very great great luxury.

title: CHAPTER XXI.
summary: The three of us had entered the fissure from the main gorge. We could not see through the whole.extent of this opening; but, as a good deal of light came down it, we had little doubt of finding at the top a clear passage into the air. The sides of the cleft we were now attempting to ascend were so excessively slippery, being wet, that we could get but little foothold upon them.

title: CHAPTER XXII.
summary: The whole country around us seemed to be swarming with savages. The vessel still lay calmly at theanchor in the bay, those on board being apparently quite unconscious of any danger awaiting them. The canoes, too, which lay at the head of the bay were manned by savages, unarmed, it is true, but who undoubtedly had arms within reach.

title: CHAPTER XXIII.
summary: During the six or seven days immediately following we remained in our hiding-place upon the hill, going out only occasionally, and then with the greatest precaution, for water and filberts. The bird we had taken in such good season proved excellent eating, although it was not an oceanic fowl, but a species of bittern. We afterward saw three of the same kind in the vicinity of the ravine.

title: CHAPTER XXIV.
summary: On the twentieth of the month, finding it altogether impossible to Subsist any longer upon the filberts, we resolved to make a desperate attempt at descending the southern declivity of the hill. After a long search we discovered a narrow ledge about twenty feet below theriminationbrink of the gulf. On this Peters contrived to leap, with what assistance I could render him by means of our pocket-handkerchiefs tied together.

title: CHAPTER XXV.
summary: Pym's final chapters have been lost through the accident by which he perished himself. It is feared that the few remaining chapters which.were to have completed his narrative, and which were retained by him, have been. lost. The figures of the chasms have a fanciful resemblance toalphabetical characters. The upper range is evidently the Arabic verbal root "To be white"
